recapPrompt.prompt: Why can sorted order make merging easier?

choices.label: Because the next smallest value is always at one of the current fronts
choices.feedback: Correct. That is why merging sorted arrays can be done in one clean pass.

choices.label: Because sorted data becomes random enough to guess
choices.feedback: Not this one. Sorted order helps because it is structured, not random.

reflectionPrompt.prompt: Explain in your own words why a rotated sorted array is still useful for smart searching.

practiceChallenge.title: Mastery check: rebuild the sort-and-search clues
practiceChallenge.description: Complete the helper so it returns the main clue for direct search, insertion sort, and rotated-array search.
hints: Linear search checks items one by one.
Insertion sort slides one value into the sorted left side.
Rotated search finds which half is still sorted.
practiceChallenge.successMessage: Pass. You rebuilt the main sort-and-search clues from memory.
practiceChallenge.retryMessage: Needs practice. Recheck direct search, insertion sort, and rotated-order search.

blanks.label: Main clue for insertion sort
expectedValues: slide into the sorted left side

blanks.label: Main clue for rotated-array search
expectedValues: find which half is still sorted

visibleTests.label: Insertion-sort clue
visibleTests.expected: slide into the sorted left side
